metadata.career_relevance: Generic interfaces are the backbone of Java enterprise frameworks. Spring Data's Repository<T, ID>, Hibernate's generic DAO, MapStruct's Mapper<S, T>, Java's Comparator<T>, Comparable<T>, Predicate<T>, Function<T,R> — all generic interfaces. After this topic, you understand HOW these frameworks are built, not just how to use them. You can build your own framework-quality generic APIs. This is senior-developer-level design knowledge.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll define generic interfaces, implement them for concrete types, use bounds on interface type parameters, and implement four real design patterns: Repository, Mapper, Strategy, and Builder — all as generic interfaces.

visual_aid.description: Show four pattern boxes side by side: Repository<T,ID> (CRUD methods + default exists()), Mapper<S,T> (mapTo/mapFrom + default mapAllTo()), Strategy<T> (algorithm method + name()), Builder<T> (build()). Each box has: interface declaration, one concrete implementation class below with arrows, and a real-world use case label. Center: the Spring Data hierarchy — CrudRepository<T,ID> → JpaRepository<T,ID> → UserRepository (extends) — showing the full chain that students now understand.
visual_aid.alt_text: Four generic design patterns: Repository, Mapper, Strategy, Builder with implementations and Spring Data hierarchy

worked_example.scenario: Build a mini e-commerce backend using all four generic interface patterns together. A generic Repository for data access, a Mapper between entity and DTO, a validation Strategy, and a Builder for creating Orders.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Build:
1. `Repository<T, ID>` interface with default methods
2. `OrderRepository implements Repository<Order, String>` — in-memory impl
3. `Mapper<Order, OrderDto>` — entity to API response
4. `Validator<T>` (strategy) — pluggable validation rule
5. `OrderBuilder implements Builder<Order>` — fluent construction

Demonstrate saving, finding, converting, and building orders.

step_by_step_solution.action: Order entity, DTO, and concrete Repository

worked_example.expected_output:
Rejected ORD-003: Amount must be positive
Saved: 2 | Count: 2
Exists ORD-001: true
DTOs: 2
Found: Dto[ORD-001,Mouse,₹799.0]
things_to_notice: Four generic interfaces work together: Repository<T,ID>, Mapper<S,T>, Validator<T>, Builder<T>. Each is a SINGLE-RESPONSIBILITY generic contract.
OrderRepository implements Repository<Order, String> — T=Order, ID=String. The type parameters become concrete when implementing the interface.
Repository.exists() is a default method — it uses findById() (also declared in the interface). Default methods let generic interfaces provide useful behavior built from their own methods.
Repository.saveAll() also a default method using stream().map(this::save) — method references work seamlessly with interface default methods.
Validator<Order> is a functional interface (one abstract method) — it can be replaced with a lambda: `Validator<Order> v = o -> o.getAmount() > 0`.
OrderMapper.mapAllTo() is inherited from the Mapper default method — 'for free'. The concrete class only needs to implement mapTo() and the bulk operation comes along.
This IS the Spring Data / MapStruct pattern. UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Integer> IS implementing Repository<User, Integer>. The framework generates the save/findById/etc.

industry_examples.use_case: `CrudRepository<T, ID>` has save(), findById(), findAll(), deleteById(), existsById(). `JpaRepository<T, ID>` extends it with flush(), saveAndFlush(), deleteAllInBatch(). When you write `interface UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Integer>`, you implement this generic interface and Spring generates the code. The interface you wrote today IS what Spring extends.

MapStruct's `Mapper` interface (with @Mapper annotation) follows the Mapper<S,T> pattern. `UserMapper` converts User to UserDto and back. MapStruct generates implementations at compile time from your interface definition. The pattern in this topic is exactly what MapStruct builds.

Internal validation frameworks use `Validator<T>` where T is the entity being validated. `OrderValidator`, `PaymentValidator`, `AddressValidator` all implement the same interface. A validation engine takes `List<Validator<T>>` — runs all validators generically. Builder patterns produce complex order/payment objects with 10+ fields safely.

applications.interview_relevance:
Senior-level generic interface questions:

- **'How is Spring Data's Repository implemented?'** → It's a generic interface `CrudRepository<T, ID>`. Spring generates the implementation. The interface declares the contract; the framework fulfills it.
- **'Implement a generic DAO interface.'** → Show Repository<T, ID> with findById, save, findAll, plus a default exists() method.
- **'What design pattern is this?'** (showing Repository) → Repository pattern — separates data access logic from business logic. Generic version allows one interface for all entities.
- **'What is a functional interface?'** → Interface with exactly one abstract method — eligible for lambda. Validator<T> with `boolean isValid(T)` is a functional interface.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Generic interfaces in daily professional work:

- Every JPA repository: `interface XyzRepository extends JpaRepository<Xyz, Long>`
- Every DTO conversion: implementing Mapper<Entity, Dto> or using MapStruct
- Validation pipelines: `List<Validator<T>>` with a loop calling isValid()
- Builder for complex objects with many optional fields
- Event handlers: `interface EventHandler<E extends Event>` for typed event processing
- Comparison strategies: `interface RankingStrategy<T>` for pluggable scoring/ranking

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Implementing a generic interface without providing type arguments — raw type.
common_gaps.remediation: Always provide concrete types when implementing: `class UserRepo implements Repository<User, Integer>` — NOT `class UserRepo implements Repository` (raw). Raw implementation loses all type safety.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Raw type warning on the class declaration

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Implementing a generic interface but not implementing all abstract methods.
common_gaps.remediation: If you don't implement all abstract methods, declare the class abstract OR implement ALL methods. Default interface methods don't need implementing. Use IDE's 'generate overrides' to scaffold all required methods.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Compile error: class must implement all abstract methods

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Not knowing that default methods in generic interfaces CAN call other interface methods (both abstract and other defaults).
common_gaps.remediation: Default methods can call any other method declared on the interface — including abstract ones (which will resolve to the implementation's version at runtime). This is how `exists()` calls `findById()` without knowing the implementation.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Unnecessarily duplicating default method logic in each implementation

key_takeaways: Generic interface syntax: `interface Name<T>` — same as generic class
Implement by providing concrete types: `class Impl implements Interface<User, Integer>`
Or keep generic: `class Impl<T, ID> implements Interface<T, ID>`
Default methods on generic interfaces: can use interface's abstract methods — 'free' behavior
Repository<T, ID>: the DAO/data-access pattern — Spring Data's foundation
Mapper<S, T>: bidirectional type conversion — MapStruct's pattern
Validator<T>: pluggable validation — functional interface, lambdas work
Builder<T>: fluent construction with type-safe build()
These four patterns cover the majority of generic interface use in production
Understanding these patterns = understanding how Spring Data, MapStruct, and Guava are built

self_check_questions.question: What is a `default` method on a generic interface and why is it useful?
self_check_questions.answer: A default method is a concrete method implementation in an interface. On a generic interface, it can call other (abstract) interface methods using `this.methodName()`. This allows adding behavior that all implementations get for free — without them having to implement it. Example: `Repository.exists(id)` calls `this.findById(id)` which will resolve to whatever the concrete implementation of findById is. New methods can be added to generic interfaces without breaking all existing implementations by making them default.

self_check_questions.question: How is `interface UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Integer>` an implementation of a generic interface?
self_check_questions.answer: JpaRepository<T, ID> is a generic interface. When UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Integer>, it provides concrete types for T (User) and ID (Integer). This is a partial implementation — UserRepository inherits all of JpaRepository's method contracts. Spring's framework provides the full concrete implementation at runtime. The pattern is: generic interface defines contract → framework generates implementation → your interface adds domain-specific methods.

self_check_questions.question: Why is `Validator<T>` a functional interface? What does that enable?
self_check_questions.answer: `Validator<T>` has exactly ONE abstract method: `boolean isValid(T item)`. This makes it a functional interface — eligible for lambda expressions. Instead of creating a named class (`class PositiveAmountValidator implements Validator<Order>`), callers can write: `Validator<Order> v = order -> order.getAmount() > 0`. This is how Java's Predicate<T>, Consumer<T>, Function<T,R> etc. work — they're functional interfaces that accept lambdas.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Module 9 is complete! You now understand generics from top to bottom: generic classes, generic methods, bounded type parameters, wildcards, and generic interfaces with real design patterns. These concepts are the foundation of Java's Collections Framework, Spring Data, and virtually every professional Java API. Module 10 covers **Concurrency and Threads** — writing code that does multiple things simultaneously, essential for backend services that handle thousands of users at once.
